coredata.dc:title: Designing nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped graphene quantum dots/g-C3N4 heterojunction composites to enhance visible and ultraviolet photocatalytic activity
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Zengsheng
Ni, Shengnan
Wu, Hao
Wen, Jianfeng
Li, Xinyu
Tang, Tao
Li, Ming
Liu, Min
coredata.dc:description: In metal-free g-C3N4 based materials, the rapid recombination of photoexcited carriers limits its photocatalytic activity. In this work, for the first time, we synthesized a new kind of diatomic-doped graphene quantum dots (GQDs), namely, nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped GQDs (NP-GQDs), and then loaded on g-C3N4 surface to obtain NP-GQDs/g-C3N4 composite photocatalyst. Under visible and ultraviolet irradiation, the MO photodegradation rate of NP-GQDs/g-C3N4 is as high as 97.5% and 96%, respectively, which was 1.71 and 1.85 times higher than that of pure g-C3N4. The remarkable enhancement of photodegradation activity is mainly attributed to the introduction of NP-GQDs could promote the formation, transportation and separation of photoexcited carriers. Furthermore, the photocatalyst exhibits outstanding recycle stability without a significant decrease in activity after three reused runs. This study provides a new understanding of the roles played by diatomic-doped graphene quantum dots in photocatalytic systems and provides a new prospect in developing highly efficient g-C3N4-based photocatalysts.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Metal-free g-C3N4
GQDs
N and P co-doping
Photodegradation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM and (b) HRTEM images of NP-GQDs. Typical TEM images of (c) g-C3N4 and (d) NP-GQDs-90/g-C3N4. (e) High-resolution image corresponding to the red rectangular area in (d). (f) HRTEM image of NP-GQDs-90/g-C3N4. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
(a) XRD patterns of g-C3N4 and NP-GQDs-90/g-C3N4, (b) FTIR spectra of NP-GQDs, NP-GQDs-90/g-C3N4 and g-C3N4.
(a) XPS, (b) C 1s and (c) N 1s high-resolution XPS spectra of NP-GQDs-90/g-C3N4 and g-C3N4. (d) P 2p high-resolution XPS spectra of NP-GQDs-90/g-C3N4.
Optical properties of g-C3N4 and NP-GQDs-90/g-C3N4. (a) UV–vis absorption spectra, (b) Tauc plots corresponding to (a), (c) Steady-state PL spectra and (d) Photocurrent responses.

Photocatalytic evaluation of all samples under (a) visible light (Xe lamp) and (b) ultraviolet light (Hg lamp). C and C0 are the current and initial MO concentrations. Kinetics of the degradation of the different samples under (c) visible and (d) ultraviolet light. (e) The reusability of NP-GQDs-90/g-C3N4 for MO removal. (f) Photocatalytic process of NP-GQDs/g-C3N4 with different NP-GQDs contents.
The schematic degradation process of NP-GQDs/g-C3N4 under visible and UV light.

head.ce:title: Designing nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped graphene quantum dots/g-C3N4 heterojunction composites to enhance visible and ultraviolet photocatalytic activity

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Science & Ministry-province jointly-constructed Cultivation Base for State Key Laboratory of Processing for Mom-ferrous Metal and Featured Materials & Key Lab. of Nonferrous Materials and New Processing Technology, Guilin University of Technology, Guilin 541004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Science & Ministry-province jointly-constructed Cultivation Base for State Key Laboratory of Processing for Mom-ferrous Metal and Featured Materials & Key Lab. of Nonferrous Materials and New Processing Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Science & Ministry-province jointly-constructed Cultivation Base for State Key Laboratory of Processing for Mom-ferrous Metal and Featured Materials & Key Lab. of Nonferrous Materials and New Processing Technology, Guilin University of Technology, Guilin 541004, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physics and Electronics, State Key Laboratory of Powder Metallurgy, Hunan Provincial Key Laboratory of Chemical Power Sources, Central South University, Changsha 410083, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Powder Metallurgy, Hunan Provincial Key Laboratory of Chemical Power Sources

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Physics and Electronics, State Key Laboratory of Powder Metallurgy, Hunan Provincial Key Laboratory of Chemical Power Sources, Central South University, Changsha 410083, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In metal-free g-C3N4 based materials, the rapid recombination of photoexcited carriers limits its photocatalytic activity. In this work, for the first time, we synthesized a new kind of diatomic-doped graphene quantum dots (GQDs), namely, nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped GQDs (NP-GQDs), and then loaded on g-C3N4 surface to obtain NP-GQDs/g-C3N4 composite photocatalyst. Under visible and ultraviolet irradiation, the MO photodegradation rate of NP-GQDs/g-C3N4 is as high as 97.5% and 96%, respectively, which was 1.71 and 1.85 times higher than that of pure g-C3N4. The remarkable enhancement of photodegradation activity is mainly attributed to the introduction of NP-GQDs could promote the formation, transportation and separation of photoexcited carriers. Furthermore, the photocatalyst exhibits outstanding recycle stability without a significant decrease in activity after three reused runs. This study provides a new understanding of the roles played by diatomic-doped graphene quantum dots in photocatalytic systems and provides a new prospect in developing highly efficient g-C3N4-based photocatalysts.

ce:section.ce:para: Semiconductor photocatalytic technology has great potential to solve the problem of dye waste pollution for its simplicity, convenience, efficiency, and environmental friendliness [1–6]. Among semiconductor photocatalysts, graphite carbon nitride (g-C3N4) is extremely fascinating due to their metal-free and non-toxicity nature [7–9]. However, its slow charge mobility and high carrier recombination rate limits its photocatalytic performance [10–12]. To improve the photocatalytic performance of g-C3N4, researchers have developed various methods, such as surface modification, atomic doping and especially, heterojunction building [13–18], since the carrier transfer and recombination process can be widely tuned in heterostructures.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), due to its unique quantum size and boundary effect [19,20], can be easily designed as a cocatalyst to match the band structure of g-C3N4 to form an efficient photocatalytic heterojunction [21–24]. Through surface electrostatic interacting effect, Liu et al modified mesoporous g-C3N4 with GQDs to improve the photodegradation activity of g-C3N4 under the visible light irradiation [25]. By atomic doping, Feng et al synthesized nitrogen-doped GQDs (N-GQDs) modified Ag2CrO4@g-C3N4 core–shell structure, achieving a full-spectrum photodegradation from UV to near-infrared (NIR) region [26]. Dong et al adopted phosphorous-doped GQDs (P-GQDs) as p-type semiconductor to form p-n junction with n-type g-C3N4, effectively promoting its pollutant degradation ability [27]. Since respective N-doped and P-doped GQDs can improve the photocatalytic activity of g-C3N4, we rationally speculate that, the N and P co-doped graphene quantum dots (NP-GQDs), will have a better performance when modified with g-C3N4.

In this work, NP-GQDs/g-C3N4 composite photocatalyst was successfully synthesized by using a hydrothermal method. The obtained heterojunction exhibits efficient photogenerated carriers transfer and separation, resulting more holes and electrons participate in the degradation of methyl orange (MO) under no matter visible or UV light. Additionally, the obtained heterojunction displays a better performance than that of single-element N or P doping. This study provides a brand-new co-doped GQDs, and it will greatly extend the application of g-C3N4-based photocatalysts.

Ammonium dihydrogen phosphate (NH4H2PO4 ≥ 99%, Guilin Baer), pyrene (C6H6 ≥ 98%, Aladdin), carbamide (H2NCONH2 ≥ 99%, Aladdin), Methyl Orange (C14H14O3N3SNa, Aladdin) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH ≥ 96%, Aladdin).

First, 0.5 g pyrene was put into 40 ml concentrated HNO3 with reflux stirring (80 °C, 12 h) and followed by drying in oven to obtain precursor trinitropyrene. Then, 40 mg trinitropyrene, 1.2 g NH4H2PO4 and 0.9 g NaOH were dispersed in 40 ml deionized water, after 0.5 h ultrasonication (500 W, 40 kHz) the solution was put in a Teflon autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 6 h. Next, the large particles and insoluble carbon products were discarded through a vacuum filtration, and the unreacted small molecules were removed by dialysis (1000 Da, 48 h). Finally, the solution in the dialysis bag was freeze-dried and NP-GQDs powder was obtained. For comparison, N-GQDs and P-GQDs were also synthesized by substituting NH4H2PO4 with ammonia and Na2HPO4, respectively.

The g-C3N4 powder was prepared by the thermal polycondensation method reported elsewhere [28]. Namely, 30 g urea powder was put into an alumina crucible with lid, and then heated at 550 °C for 3 h using a heating rate of 2.5 °C min−1 in muffle furnace. After cooled to room temperature, the light-yellow g-C3N4 powder was acquired.

NP-GQDs/g-C3N4 photocatalyst was obtained by mixing NP-GQDs and g-C3N4. First, 0.3 g g-C3N4 was dissolved in 80 ml deionized water. Subsequently, the X mg NP-GQDs was dispersed in g-C3N4 aqueous solution (X = 60, 90 and 120) with ultrasonication for 0.5 h. Then the 100 ml mixed solution was placed in a Teflon autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 6 h. After cooling, the samples were cleaned with ultrapure water and ethanol, then dried in oven for 12 h. The final product was named as NP-GQDs-X/g-C3N4.

The crystal structures of the samples were investigated with a MiniFlex-600 (JEOL, Japan) X-ray diffractometer (XRD). The morphology measurements of the samples were conducted with a JEM-2100F (Tokyo, Japan) field emission transmission electron microscope (TEM). The surface analyses of the samples were investigated with an ESCALAB-250XI (Waltham, USA) X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). UV–vis absorption spectra, Photoluminescence (PL) spectra and Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy spectra were respectively determined using a PerkinElmer Lambda 750 spectrophotometer (CT, USA), Edinburgh FL/FS900 Carry Eclipse (Cheadle, UK) and NICOLET-6700 FTIR spectrometer (Tokyo, Japan).

The photocatalytic activity of the samples was investigated by testing the degradation of methyl orange (MO) under a light illumination. Xe lamp (PLS-SXE300, 300 W, λ > 420 nm) and Hg lamp (BL-GHX-V, 500 W) were adopted as visible and UV light source, respectively. The 50 mg sample was placed in 50 ml MO solution (10 mg/L), and the mixed solution was stirred for 0.5 h at dark to achieve an adsorption–desorption equilibrium of MO on the sample surface. Every 0.5 h (under visible light) or 2 min (under UV light), 4 ml solution was taken out to determine its MO concentration with a Lambda 950 UV–vis spectrophotometer.

The transient photocurrent measurements were carried out with a three-electrode electrochemical workstation (CHI760E, Chenhua, Shanghai). Na2SO4 (0.2 M) was used as the electrolyte solution. Pt film and saturated calomel electrode (SCE) were used as the counter and reference electrodes, respectively. The working electrode was a 0.8 × 0.8 × 0.1 cm3 nickel foam coated with 9 mg sample fully mixed with 1 mg PVDF. After drying at 80 °C for 4 h, the coating thickness is assumed to be ~0.1 mm. The light source is adopted as Xe lamp (PLS-SXE300, 300 W, λ > 420 nm).

Fig. 1 shows the TEM and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images of NP-GQDs, g-C3N4 and NP-GQDs-90/g-C3N4. NP-GQDs have a relatively uniform size distribution (~3 nm) and a clear fringe spacing of 0.242 nm (Fig. 1a & b), corresponding to the (1120) facet of sp2 graphitic carbon in graphene [29,30]. Fig. 1c & d exhibit the typical 2D agglomerated lamellar-like morphology of g-C3N4. After the modification of NP-GQDs, its morphology has no obvious change only with the successful decoration of NP-GQDs on the surface (Fig. 1e). Fig. 1f shows a HRTEM image of NP-GQDs-90/g-C3N4. The different crystal structures of the two composites can be clearly resolved.

The crystal structures of g-C3N4 and NP-GQDs-90/g-C3N4 were analyzed by XRD. As shown in Fig. 2 a, g-C3N4 has two strong peaks at 12.98° (100) and 27.4° (002), which are ascribed to the in-plane structural accumulation of tri-s-triazine units and the interlayer stacking of aromatic segments, respectively [31–33]. When NP-GQDs was loaded on the surface of g-C3N4, the characteristic peak of NP-GQDs-90/g-C3N4 composite was identical with that of g-C3N4, indicating that NP-GQDs exerts no obvious influence on the crystal structure of g-C3N4. We also obtained XRD and Raman spectra of NP-GQDs (Fig. S1), and the both results can show the typical features originated from graphene.

The surface groups of NP-GQDs, g-C3N4 and NP-GQDs-90/g-C3N4 were analyzed by FTIR (Fig. 2b). The absorption peaks of pure g-C3N4 and NP-GQDs/g-C3N4 at 1637, 1408, 1324 and 1238 cm−1 are ascribed to the conjugated g-C3N4 heterocycle, and the absorption peak at 814 cm−1 results from the tri-s-triazine unit. In addition, the 3000–3500 cm−1 broad absorption peaks are caused by the stretching vibrations from N-H and –OH. Compared with g-C3N4, the distinct extra absorption peaks of NP-GQDs/g-C3N4 in 1065 cm−1 are ascribed to the stretching modes of POC originated from NP-GQDs, indicating the successful surface coupling between NP-GQDs and g-C3N4. Other featured absorption peaks ascribed to NP-GQDs are not distinguishable in NP-GQDs/g-C3N4, it may be due to the smaller mass fraction of NP-GQDs relative to g-C3N4.

The XPS analyses of NP-GQDs are shown in Fig. S2. It demonstrates N and P atoms are successfully doped into GQDs, and the atomic ratios of N/C and P/C are 0.07 and 0.46, respectively (Table S1), which are in accordance with the elemental analysis results (Table S2). The doping forms are mainly pyridinic, pyrrolic and graphitic N for N-doping, and PO and PC for P-doping (Table S3). Fig. 3 a shows the XPS results of g-C3N4 and NP-GQDs-90/g-C3N4. The oxygen peak in g-C3N4 is probably attributed to the adsorbed H2O on the surface [34]. Compared with the pure g-C3N4, two new peaks positioned at 132.9 and 190.1 eV, corresponding to P 2p and P 2s, were observed in NP-GQDs-90/g-C3N4. In Fig. 3b, three major peaks at 284.4 (CC), 287.8 (CN) and 288.1 eV (NCN2) over C 1s spectra are respectively attributed to the aromatic carbon atoms, sp3-coordinated carbon bonds and sp2-bonded carbon [35]. In addition, a new peak at 285.1 eV over C 1s spectrum of NP-GQDs-90/g-C3N4 can be attributed to the oxygen-containing functional groups (COH) from NP-GQDs. In Fig. 3c, four major peaks at 398.7 (CNC), 399.9 (NC3), 401.3 (CNH2) and 404.6 eV (excitation N) over N 1s spectra of NP-GQDs-90/g-C3N4 and g-C3N4 can be assigned to the triazine rings, tertiary N groups, amino groups and the excitations, respectively [36]. Note that the surface coupling between NP-GQDs-90 and g-C3N4 slightly shifts the binding energies of some bonds (the dash line in Fig. 3b & c). In Fig. 3d, two major peaks at 132.9 eV and 133.8 eV over P 2p spectra of NP-GQDs-90/g-C3N4 are attributed to PO and PC bonds.

The UV–vis absorption spectra of the samples are shown in Fig. 4 a. The pure g-C3N4 possesses a clear absorption edge at around 450 nm [37]. When NP-GQDs was loaded on g-C3N4, a new absorption band appears at around 635 nm, and the absorption of the composites was enhanced in the whole wavelength range. Especially, in the visible range (400–650 nm), its absorbance has been improved by ~3 times, indicating the better responsibility of the visible light. The Tauc equation was used to obtain the band gap information. We obtain the band gaps of g-C3N4 and NP-GQDs-90/g-C3N4 are 2.71 and 2.68 eV, respectively (Fig. 4b). After the incorporation of NP-GQDs, the band gap of g-C3N4 is slightly narrower, proving that the NP-GQDs-90/g-C3N4 composite can utilize more visible light.

The photoluminescence emission spectra and transient photocurrent response of samples were conducted to study the recombination and separation of photoexcited carriers [38,39]. As seen in Fig. 4c, under the same excitation of 370 nm, both samples have a broad emission peak at 400–600 nm, however, the peak intensity of NP-GQDs-90/g-C3N4 composite is significantly lower than that of pure g-C3N4. Considering the stronger absorption of NP-GQDs-90/g-C3N4 than g-C3N4, it suggests that NP-GQDs/g-C3N4 photogenerates more carriers while has less e-h direct recombination to emit PL. We performed transient photocurrent measurements under a visible light to further assess the carrier separation and transferring efficiency (Fig. 4d). It is clear, the photocurrent of g-C3N4 is obviously enhanced after NP-GQDs loading (increased from 0.24 to 0.45 μA/cm2), demonstrating a better separation of photogenerated carries and enhanced light utilization of NP-GQDs-90/g-C3N4.

The photodegradation performance of all catalysts was mainly evaluated by the concentration of MO. As shown in Fig. 5 a, without the catalysts, MO is barely degraded. After 120 min illumination under visible light, the pure g-C3N4, GQDs/g-C3N4, N-GQDs/g-C3N4 and P-GQDs/g-C3N4 depict a photocatalytic degradation ratio of ~56.8%, 69%, 93.6% and 94.7%, respectively (Fig. 5b). Noteworthily, NP-GQDs-90/g-C3N4 has a higher degradation rate of ~97.5% compared with those of other samples. Under UV light, all the samples can degrade MO faster, ~ 52.7%, 68.4%, 81.8%, 85% and 96% MO has been removed by g-C3N4, GQDs/g-C3N4, N-GQDs/g-C3N4, P-GQDs/g-C3N4 and NP-GQDs-90/g-C3N4 in 8 min, respectively. No matter under visible or UV light, the degradation velocity of NP-GQDs-90/g-C3N4 is over 4 times faster than pure g-C3N4 (Fig. 5c &d). Compared with the GQDs-based photocatalysts developed by other groups (Table S4), NP-GQDs/g-C3N4 also can be seemed as a strong competitor to fast and effectively photodegrade pollutants. As shown in Fig. 5e, after three cycles, the photodegradation ability of the NP-GQDs-90/g-C3N4 catalyst remains quite stable, indicating it can be repeatedly used in MO degradation.

The photodegradation ability of g-C3N4 is not monotonously increased with increasing the NP-GQDs coverage. NP-GQDs-90/g-C3N4 has a better performance than NP-GQDs-60/g-C3N4 and NP-GQDs-120/g-C3N4. Fig. 5f gives a probable explanation about such phenomenon. When NP-GQDs content is small, higher NP-GQDs coverage means more photogenerated charges transfer between NP-GQDs and g-C3N4 to reduce direct PL-emissive recombination, namely, more electrons and holes can participate in the MO degradation process. However, if the NP-GQDs content surpasses a critical value, too much light is absorbed by NP-GQDs and not enough photoinduced carries could be generated in g-C3N4, then the number of carriers which take part in photodegradation would decrease to lower the photocatalytic efficiency. Moreover, too high coverage of NP-GQDs may prohibit some active sites on g-C3N4 react with MO, and further decrease the photocatalytic efficiency. In any case, by choosing appropriate coverage of NP-GQDs, we can remarkably improve the photodegradation ability of g-C3N4, no matter under visible or UV light.

Fig. 6 shows the photodegradation mechanism of the samples under visible and UV light. Figs. 4, S3 and S4 give the energy band structures of NP-GQDs and g-C3N4, and they can form a type II heterojunction, in which the valance band (VB) maximum of NP-GQDs is lying in the band gap of g-C3N4 and its conduction band (CB) minimum is higher than which of g-C3N4. When NP-GQDs/g-C3N4 is irradiated by UV light, both NP-GQDs and g-C3N4 can photogenerate e-h pairs. Driven by the energy level difference of NP-GQDs and g-C3N4, photogenerated electrons accumulate on the CB of g-C3N4 to turn adsorbed O2 into •O2−, and holes on the VB of NP-GQDs to turn adsorbed water into •OH. These active groups will oxidize MO into CO2 and H2O, and then MO is photodegraded. The fast photodegradation of NP-GQDs/g-C3N4 indicates that such a type II heterojunction can transfer the photogenerated carriers rapidly, thus reducing the direct e-h recombination and making more free carriers join the pollutant decomposition process. Under the short wavelength (λ < 450 nm) visible light, the photocatalytic process is totally similar to UV light, since the excitation photon energy is still high enough to exceed the band gaps of NP-GQDs and g-C3N4. For the long wavelength light (λ > 450 nm), we also believe that a part of light (for instance, λ 〈650) can be used for MO photodegradation because both the broad absorption (Fig. 4a) and PL spectrum (Fig. 4c) of NP-GQDs/g-C3N4 indicate that e-h pairs also could be generated under such excitation.

In summary, we synthesized a new photocatalyst NP-GQDs/g-C3N4 by loading NP-GQDs on g-C3N4. The obtained heterojunction can rapidly transfer photogenerated holes or electrons to each other, thus reducing the direct recombination of the photogenerated carriers and making more carriers used to degrade pollutants. The MO photodegradation rate of NP-GQDs-90/g-C3N4 is as high as 97.5% under visible light irradiation for 120 min, and 96% under UV light irradiation for only 8 min, much faster than pure g-C3N4. Moreover, the NP-GQDs/g-C3N4 also exhibits outstanding recycle stability. Our work provides a novel co-doped GQDs which can significantly improve the photodegradation ability of g-C3N4, and we highlight NP-GQDs/g-C3N4 is an efficient, metal-free and highly stable photocatalyst.

Zengsheng Guo: Investigation, Writing - original draft. Shengnan Ni: Investigation, Data curation. Hao Wu: Investigation. Jianfeng Wen: Funding acquisition, Resources. Xinyu Li: Funding acquisition, Resources. Tao Tang: Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Ming Li: Funding acquisition, Supervision. Min Liu: Funding acquisition, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
